stories.title: Asiatic Wild Ass Returns to Eastern Mongolia After 65-year Isolation from Landscape Fencing
stories.summary: After 65 years away, Asiatic wild asses are returning to eastern Mongolia. Fence removal and safe corridors along the Trans-Mongolian Railway are helping them recolonize their ancestral lands.
stories.extended_summary: For six decades, fencing along the Trans-Mongolian Railway kept the khulan (Asiatic wild ass) locked out of eastern Mongolia. Conservation groups worked with the government to remove stretches of fence and designate a safe passage zone. Now hundreds of khulan have crossed and are starting to re-establish. Scientists call it an extraordinary conservation breakthrough.
stories.headline_split: Asiatic Wild Ass Returns to Eastern Mongolia After 65-year Isolation
stories.fake_headline: Endangered Sea Turtle Population Reaches Record High After Decade-Long Conservation Effort

stories.title: Breck’s old-school fire chief is sold on new-school AI cameras for wildfire
stories.summary: AI-powered cameras are helping Summit County firefighters spot and extinguish wildfires before they grow. The fire chief calls them a game changer.
stories.extended_summary: Five AI cameras installed by Xcel Energy now scan Summit County for smoke and hot spots. Fire Chief Drew Hoehn says they've cut response times dramatically, often catching fires before the public even knows they're burning. With hundreds of small starts every season, early detection means these never become the 20-30 fires that earn names.
stories.headline_split: Breck’s old-school fire chief is sold on new-school
stories.fake_headline: Old-school librarian uses AI to digitize rare books and make them accessible online

stories.title: Brother-Sister Duo Are Using Drones to Help Farmers Triple Their Income
stories.summary: Siblings Devika and Devan Chandrasekharan built drones that can spot plant diseases early and spray only where needed — helping over 3,500 farmers boost yields by up to 35% and triple their profits while cutting fertilizer use by 75%.
stories.extended_summary: Brother-sister duo Devika and Devan Chandrasekharan started Fuselage Innovations in 2020 with a simple mission: use drones to make farming smarter. Their drones Niriksh and Fiya work together — Niriksh scans fields with sensors to detect pests and nutrient deficiencies, and Fiya precisely sprays only the affected plants. After working with 3,500 farmers, they've seen yields jump 25-35%, fertilizer use drop 75%, and profits triple. For small farmers in Kerala, these drones aren't just cool tech — they're a lifeline to sustainable income.

stories.title: Clinics at scale: How Ilara Health is building Africa's quiet health infrastructure
stories.summary: Emilian Popa runs a healthtech company that does not behave like one. While investors spent years debating whether African healthtech could scale sustainably, Ilara Health concentrated on a narrower and more practical problem: small private clinics lack diagnostic equipment because they cannot afford to finance it. The company’s response has been straightforward. It supplies clinics with medical e
stories.extended_summary: Emilian Popa runs a healthtech company that does not behave like one. While investors spent years debating whether African healthtech could scale sustainably, Ilara Health concentrated on a narrower and more practical problem: small private clinics lack diagnostic equipment because they cannot afford to finance it. The company’s response has been straightforward. It supplies clinics with medical equipment on credit, layers in software and data tools, and gradually turns fragmented operators into part of a functioning network. The model is beginning to achieve meaningful scale. Ilara says it has now served more than two million patients across around 3,000 clinics in Kenya and Uganda, a segment long considered too fragmented and informal to organise effectively. stories.headline_split: Clinics at scale: How Ilara Health is building Africa's quiet health

stories.title: Colorado nonprofit offers to buy water for fish, a deal that could help farms weather record drought
stories.summary: Colorado Water Trust is buying water for fish during the drought, using a new law that allows water to be used for environmental purposes. It could also help farmers earn cash from spare water.
stories.extended_summary: As drought drains Colorado's streams, the Colorado Water Trust is trying something new: buying water from owners and putting it back into rivers for fish. New state laws allow water to be used for the environment. The trust has already restored over 98,000 acre-feet. Executive director Kate Ryan calls the drought unprecedented but sees opportunity for ranchers to sell spare water — a win for farms and fish alike.

stories.title: He quit his senior engineering job, moved back with his parents, and built Kleo from zero to $62K MRR in 90 days
stories.summary: Cameron Whiteside left his senior engineering job in London, moved back in with his parents, and built a Chrome extension called Kleo that helps people write better LinkedIn content. With co-founders who had massive social audiences, Kleo hit $62K monthly recurring revenue in just 90 days.
stories.extended_summary: Cameron Whiteside made a bold bet: quit his senior engineering role, move back into his parents' house, and build a startup with near-zero burn. The result was Kleo, a Chrome extension that makes writing LinkedIn content easier. But the real genius was distribution: he partnered with co-founders who already had hundreds of thousands of followers, turning their organic reach into free customer acquisition. In under 90 days, Kleo hit $62K in monthly recurring revenue. His takeaway? 'Audience compounds faster than product when the product is small enough to grasp in one sentence.'

stories.title: Japan built robot wolves to thwart bear attack, and they’re flying off the shelves
stories.summary: Japan's glowing robot wolves, originally for deer, are now scaring bears away as attacks surge. Demand is so high buyers wait three months.
stories.extended_summary: Ohta Seiki's 'Monster Wolf' robot has glowing red eyes, moving head, and blasts wolf howls and industrial noise. It was built to scare deer but now defends neighborhoods from bears. CCTV shows bears and boars fleeing on sight. With Japan seeing over 50,000 bear sightings, the robots are flying off the shelves — and the waitlist is three months long.

stories.title: More public restrooms will get diaper changing tables after Arlington state senator’s efforts
stories.summary: Virginia will require diaper changing tables in new public buildings, after a state senator who struggled to find them pushed for the change.
stories.extended_summary: State Sen. Elizabeth Bennett-Parker was in a restaurant with her daughter and couldn't find a changing table. That frustration became a policy win. Virginia's Board of Housing voted to require changing tables in new public buildings — including hotels and rest stops — on both male and female restrooms. The rule takes effect in 2027, giving caregivers a clean, safe place to change their babies.
stories.headline_split: More public restrooms will get diaper changing tables

stories.title: Pastor Jamal Bryant Converts 35 Church Acres Into 390 Affordable Homes For Black Families
stories.summary: Pastor Jamal Bryant is converting 35 acres of church land into 390 affordable homes for Black families in Atlanta, aiming to build generational wealth through homeownership.
stories.extended_summary: New Birth Missionary Baptist Church is turning debt-free land into New Birth Village — 390 homes affordable for teachers, nurses, and first-time buyers. Pastor Jamal Bryant launched the project with faith-based partners. The development includes mixed-use spaces and senior housing. The goal: turn renters into owners and create lasting wealth for Black families across the region.

stories.title: Pennsylvania greenhouse stays warm by storing heat underground
stories.summary: Tim Clymer built a climate battery greenhouse that stores heat underground during sunny days and releases it when cold, allowing year-round citrus growing without propane. He now shares his design to help other farmers adopt this sustainable method.
stories.extended_summary: On warm days, fans blow hot air into underground tubes, heating the soil like a thermal battery. When temperatures drop, the system reverses, drawing stored heat back into the greenhouse. Clymer has grown figs, Meyer lemons, and satsumas for nine years without burning fossil fuels, and his company now helps other farmers build their own climate batteries.
stories.headline_split: Pennsylvania greenhouse stays warm by
fake_endings: tapping into a nearby hot spring

stories.title: Shared rent and free classes: How a new community center in Five Points gets it done
stories.summary: Bruno Tapia Garcia opened the Colorado People's Center with 70 volunteers splitting the rent, offering affordable space for grassroots groups and free classes, clinics, and an art gallery. The center fills a critical need for accessible community spaces in Denver.
stories.extended_summary: Garcia noticed grassroots groups struggled to find affordable meeting spaces, so he signed a lease and rallied 70 volunteers to share the monthly rent. Electricians volunteered to renovate for free, and the 6,000-square-foot building now houses over 20 rooms, a craft closet, and a lending library. The center opened in April with a mission-first approach and no funding from groups outside their values.
stories.headline_split: Shared rent and free classes: How a new community center in Five Points
stories.fake_headline: A small library in rural Kentucky now offers free Wi-Fi via donated satellites

stories.title: The new TB diagnostic that could replace 150 years of microscope testing
stories.summary: A portable $300 device called MiniDock MTB can detect tuberculosis using a tongue swab, replacing a 150-year-old microscope test that misses half of cases. A new study across Africa and Asia verified its accuracy, offering hope for faster, more accessible diagnosis.
stories.extended_summary: For 150 years, TB diagnosis required a phlegm sample examined under a microscope, but many patients—especially children and the elderly—can't produce phlegm. The MiniDock MTB heats and spins a swab or sample to scan for TB DNA, costing just $3–$4 per test. In a study of nearly 1,400 patients across seven countries, it met WHO accuracy targets, potentially saving millions of lives by catching cases earlier.

stories.title: 44-acre solar farm to be built in Wrangell
stories.summary: A 44-acre solar farm is coming to Wrangell, Alaska, aiming to replace expensive diesel power during maintenance outages. The project starts at 1.5 megawatts and promises to slash costs and boost energy resilience for the island community.
stories.extended_summary: Wrangell burns diesel for at least a week each June, costing around $250,000. A new solar farm will offset that entirely. Borough manager Mason Villarma calls it 'huge for economic development.' The farm will start at 1.5 MW and expand to 5 MW with battery storage, keeping lights on during outages. It complements the town's existing hydropower and prepares Wrangell for future growth.
stories.headline_split: 44-acre solar farm to be built
stories.fake_headline: City Unveils Plan to Convert Old Landfill into Community Garden and Park

stories.title: 978 Jerseys gives SDSU athletes another NIL lane, one sale at a time
stories.summary: 978 Jerseys lets San Diego State athletes from all sports sell their own numbered jerseys. The company has paid over $12,000 in royalties to SDSU athletes, giving lesser-known players a piece of the NIL pie.
stories.extended_summary: For most SDSU athletes, NIL isn't a six-figure check. 978 Jerseys, founded by Ethan Chandler, allows athletes from every sport to sell jerseys with their name and number. Starting with SDSU, it has expanded to seven schools and paid out over $12,000. 'It's not life-changing money,' Chandler says, but it gives athletes something they never had: their own merchandise. A parent wearing a jersey in the stands becomes a real source of income and pride.
stories.headline_split: 978 Jerseys gives SDSU athletes

stories.title: A Denver Nonprofit Is Moving Homeless Youth to Permanent Housing Three Times Faster
stories.summary: Denver's The Mothership, a youth homeless shelter, uses a Housing First model that moves kids into permanent housing three times faster than traditional shelters. It's cheaper, too, offering therapy, medical care, and a sense of family.
stories.extended_summary: C.J. Kesner, 19, had bounced between relatives' homes until he found The Mothership. Six months later, he has a bed, a therapist, and a case manager working on housing. The shelter's Housing First model is startlingly effective: it moves youth into permanent housing three times faster and at lower cost. CEO Christina Carlson calls it a reinvention of shelter that should be replicated everywhere. Residents feel welcomed and supported for the first time.

stories.title: A Latino grocery store in Delaware turns its produce aisle into a music stage
stories.summary: Fiesta Fresh Market in Delaware turns its produce aisle into a music stage every month. Bands like Luna Luna perform among bananas and beans, drawing international fans and proving joy thrives in unexpected places.
stories.extended_summary: At Fiesta Fresh Market, you can buy a banana and catch a live band. The Mercadito sessions are the owners' love letter to music and culture—an alternative pop band plays surrounded by stacked fruit. Started by Jose Luis Aguilar Garcia, these shows have gone viral, with bands traveling to perform. It's a defiant celebration of Latino joy at a time when the community is often discussed in terms of crisis. 'We wanted to highlight creativity and happiness,' Garcia says.

stories.title: A Quiet Uprising Against Chatbots?
stories.summary: When SameSame Collective tested its simple chatbot against ChatGPT for queer youth mental health, they expected users to prefer the AI. Instead, young people rated the simpler bot higher on trust — suggesting that 'human-like' AI might actually backfire.
stories.extended_summary: Jonathan McKay was sure ChatGPT would make his nonprofit's chatbot obsolete. SameSame Collective provides mental health support to queer youth in underserved regions via WhatsApp, and when they ran a workshop comparing their original bot to an LLM-powered one, he braced for the worst. But users overwhelmingly preferred the simpler, less 'human-like' bot — scoring it higher on trustworthiness and local relevance. The finding upends assumptions about AI in social services: sometimes, trying to sound too human can break the connection.

stories.title: America’s most important innovation happens in City Hall
stories.summary: Port St. Lucie's chief innovation officer listened to residents and launched a conservation program that preserved 5,000 acres and built 20 miles of trails. A model for how city halls can drive real change.
stories.extended_summary: Kate Parmelee, Port St. Lucie's innovation officer, assumed residents wanted more parks—but they wanted nature protected. After listening to Boy Scouts, environmentalists, and skeptics, the city launched a green land bank. The result: over 5,000 acres preserved and 20 miles of new trails. Mayor Shannon Martin calls it a 'resident-driven conservation program.' This isn't just about green space; it's about identity. The story shows how civic innovation can come from simply asking what people truly need.
stories.headline_split: America’s most important innovation happens

stories.title: Australia is on track to eliminate a form of cancer entirely
stories.summary: Australia is on track to eliminate cervical cancer by 2035 through widespread HPV vaccination and a revamped screening system. It's the first time a country has targeted elimination of an entire cancer, setting a global precedent.
stories.extended_summary: Professor Karen Canfell calls it a historic first: a nation aiming to wipe out a cancer entirely. Australia launched HPV vaccination in 2007, extended it to boys in 2013, and replaced pap smears with more sensitive HPV testing in 2017. Recent self-sampling options removed barriers for underserved women. If current trends hold, Australia will reach the WHO elimination target years ahead of schedule.
stories.headline_split: Australia is on track to eliminate
stories.fake_headline: Norway opens world's largest floating wind farm off its coast

stories.title: Bellevue Just Outsmarted Some Beavers with a Beaver Deceiver
stories.summary: Bellevue Utilities deployed a Beaver Deceiver—a trapezoidal fence that tricks beavers into abandoning their dam-building. The clever invention, first used by the Penobscot Indian Nation, works by confusing the beavers' instincts so they give up and move on.
stories.extended_summary: A family of beavers thinks their dam is still working, but thanks to a 40-foot trapezoidal fence called a Beaver Deceiver, the water flows freely. Invented in the 1990s by Skip Lisle, the fence confuses beavers by forcing them to build away from the culvert, widening the stream opening as they try. The result? Beavers lose interest and relocate—no harm done, just outsmarted.

stories.fake_headline: Tennessee man finds $10,000 diamond at state park and returns it to owner

stories.title: Bill to block publishers from killing online games advances in California
stories.summary: California's Protect Our Games Act advanced out of committee, requiring game publishers to refund players or keep games playable if online support ends. The bill is a win for the Stop Killing Games movement, pushing back against industry lobbyists.
stories.extended_summary: When publishers shut down online games, players lose access entirely—but California's bill would change that. If passed, digital games sold after January 2027 must either offer refunds or an updated version that works independently. The 11–2 committee vote came after grassroots advocacy from Stop Killing Games, formed after Ubisoft's The Crew was killed. Free games and subscription titles are exempt, but paid games would finally have consumer protections.
stories.headline_split: Bill to block publishers from killing online games
stories.fake_headline: Portland creates 'tiny forest' in parking lot to combat heat island effect

stories.title: Cougar Buddies promote inclusion on and off campus
stories.summary: After starting a club to bridge gaps between general and special education students, Christopher High School's Cougar Buddies hosted Gilroy's first sensory-friendly prom. Over 50 students with special needs danced in dimmed lights and lowered music, feeling celebrated and included.
stories.extended_summary: What began as a small club at Christopher High School grew into a movement when Cougar Buddies organized Gilroy's first prom designed for students with special needs. The event welcomed over 50 attendees from two districts, with dimmed lights, low music, and carefully chosen food to make everyone comfortable. Parents tearfully thanked the student organizers for giving their children a night of belonging. The club's president, Isabel Gonzalez, says their goal is to change the stigma and separation of special needs students—and they're doing it, one dance at a time.

stories.title: Grace Chang founded Kintsugi to detect mental-health conditions from voice — AI biomarkers as a clinical screening tool
stories.summary: Grace Chang founded Kintsugi after watching her family navigate undiagnosed depression. The AI listens to just 20 seconds of speech—pitch, cadence, microtremors—to screen for depression and anxiety. It's now used in clinics nationwide, replacing the outdated paper PHQ-9.
stories.extended_summary: What if a 20-second recording of your voice could flag depression or anxiety before you even realized it? That's the promise of Kintsugi, an AI founded by Grace Chang after her own family's struggle with undiagnosed mental health issues. The tool analyzes speech patterns—microtremors, hesitation length, pitch shifts—that are invisible to the human ear but highly detectable to a trained model. Now integrated into health systems across the US, Kintsugi replaces the decades-old PHQ-9 paper screener with a faster, more objective clinical screening score. Just ask 'how was your week?' and the AI does the rest.
stories.headline_split: Grace Chang founded Kintsugi to detect mental-health conditions from voice —

stories.title: How to Grow Apples in Hot Climate? Award-Winning Farmers Reap Success With Unique Variety
stories.summary: Maharashtra farmer Kakasaheb Sawant grows apples in scorching 43°C heat using the low-chill HRMN-99 variety, developed by Himachal farmer Hariman Sharma. Visitors call it a miracle as the trees thrive against all odds.
stories.extended_summary: In Sangli's dry, sandy-loam soil with only 30 rainy days a year, Sawant's apple trees are laden with fruit. The HRMN-99 variety needs far less winter chill than traditional apples, allowing cultivation in hot climates. Sawant, a former auto mechanic, bought 150 saplings in 2020 after people laughed at his plan. Now his farm draws a steady stream of amazed visitors, proving that innovation can beat the heat.

stories.title: It’s a barracuda! It’s a shrimp! It’s a robot helping coral reefs.
stories.summary: The Woods Hole Oceanographic Institution's CUREE robot autonomously surveys coral reefs using hydrophones and cameras, even tracking a barracuda for 984 feet. The robot creates detailed maps much faster than human divers, aiding reef conservation.
stories.extended_summary: CUREE zeroes in on the crackle of shrimp and the movement of fish, then autonomously follows them to map biodiversity hotspots. In tests off the U.S. Virgin Islands, it trailed a barracuda for over 984 feet with minimal human help. The robot uses onboard computers to decide where to go, building data-rich maps that would take divers multiple trips to produce. This technology could revolutionize how we monitor and protect fragile coral ecosystems.

stories.title: Kansas Gives Foster Youth a New Way To Choose Their Forever Family
stories.summary: Kansas just became the first state to let foster youth aged 16+ choose their own legal family. Through the SOUL Family program, teens can pick trusted adults—a coach, aunt, or former foster parent—to become their permanent support system, giving them the stability they deserve.
stories.extended_summary: For decades, foster youth had only three options: reunification, adoption, or aging out. Kansas changed that with SOUL Family Legal Permanency—the first program of its kind in the nation. Now, teens 16 and older can identify the adults they already trust—like a coach, sibling, or teacher—to become their legal family. Alexandria Ware, who spent time in foster care herself, helped advocate for the law. 'It's the young person telling you, “These are my people, and this is where I feel safe,”' she said. The program shifts power from the system to the youth, letting them define their forever connections.
stories.headline_split: Kansas Gives Foster Youth a New Way To Choose
stories.fake_headline: Michigan Offers Free Pet Adoptions to Seniors Living Alone, Reunites Dozens

stories.title: Nine veterans honored ahead of burial at sea at nation’s only Veterans Memorial Reef
stories.summary: Nine veterans were honored ahead of burial at sea at the nation's only Veterans Memorial Reef off North Carolina. The reef markers create an underwater memorial that supports marine life and addresses the shortage of space in military cemeteries.
stories.extended_summary: The Fallen Heroes Ceremony at Fort Johnston–Southport Museum honored nine veterans whose remains will be placed in markers five miles offshore. The reef, founded by Army veteran Thomas Marcinowski, serves as both a tribute and an artificial habitat that protects the coastline. For Rhonda Rardin, watching her parents—married over 60 years—be interred together was a perfect sendoff. The program helps families find a lasting, meaningful resting place while giving back to the ocean.
stories.headline_split: Nine veterans honored ahead of burial at sea at

stories.title: No Fools, Just Gold
stories.summary: West Virginia University's Blue and Gold Mine Sale is a massive thrift event where 25 tons of donated goods are sold, promoting reuse and raising funds. Early birds line up for deals on furniture, electronics, and rare books.
stories.extended_summary: Every year, students moving out donate tons of items—from mini fridges to china sets—for WVU's sustainability-driven sale. Early bird shoppers arrive over an hour early, with lines of 600 to 800 people. The sale has unearthed rare books that were later resold online, and unsold items go to partner nonprofits. It's one of the largest rummage sales in the state, turning waste into treasure and funding community projects.

stories.title: Ocean City Was Sick of Greedy Seagulls. Their Eccentric Fix? A Fleet of Raptors
stories.summary: Tired of seagulls stealing snacks from beachgoers, Ocean City hired a team of falconers to patrol the skies with trained raptors. The program, launched after a gull grabbed pizza from a toddler, has been a soaring success every summer since.
stories.extended_summary: When a seagull yanked a slice of pizza from a toddler on the Ocean City boardwalk, Mayor Jay Gillian had reached his limit. After failing with fishing line and plastic owls, the city tried a wild solution: a professional falconer team releasing hawks, falcons, and even a Eurasian eagle owl to scare gulls away. The raptors have turned the tide, driving the aggressive birds back to their natural habitats without harming them—and giving locals their fries back.

stories.title: Seabed life triples after bottom trawling ban in Scotland protected area
stories.summary: A decade after Scotland banned bottom trawling in a protected area, scientists found seafloor life has tripled. Over 150 species are thriving, from spoon worms to tower snails, rebuilding a lost ecosystem and storing carbon in the process.
stories.extended_summary: The seafloor off Scotland's Arran island was once a muddy desert from centuries of trawling. Now, nearly ten years after a ban, a new study reveals that seabed life has exploded—three times more organisms and twice as many species compared to nearby unprotected waters. Lead researcher Ben Harris describes creatures like spoon worms and tower snails as 'important gardeners of the seabed,' turning over sediment and storing carbon. It's a slow but powerful recovery that shows conservation can work.

stories.title: Such great heights: They’re tall, they’re proud — and they’re getting together
stories.summary: SEATTLE (AP) — This story has legs. Very long ones. At a Seattle sports bar on a recent Saturday night, hundreds of very tall people got to experience something rare: blending in. Women in their highest heels craned their necks to look at someone taller. Men who usually duck under doorways looked ordinary. For once, nobody had to explain why they don’t play basketball. stories.extended_summary: SEATTLE (AP) — This story has legs. Very long ones. At a Seattle sports bar on a recent Saturday night, hundreds of very tall people got to experience something rare: blending in. Women in their highest heels craned their necks to look at someone taller. Men who usually duck under doorways looked ordinary. For once, nobody had to explain why they don’t play basketball. Welcome to Tall Tour, a traveling meetup drawing thousands across the United States to celebrate the one physical trait that has made them stand out — for good and ill. Since launching last summer, the tour has visited 19 cities, with crowds swelling from an initial 30 people in Tampa, Florida, to some 4,000 in Orlando, according to organizers. stories.title: The Rise In Demand For Cooling Sparks Interest In More Energy Efficient Air Conditioners
stories.summary: An MIT spinoff called Transaera is making massive air conditioning units that use 40% less energy. Their secret? Removing moisture separately from cooling, so buildings stay comfortable without wasting power.
stories.extended_summary: Global cooling demand could triple by 2050, but one startup has a smarter way to chill. Transaera’s rooftop AC units combine heat pumps with cutting-edge MOF technology that pulls humidity out of the air before cooling it. That means a room at 80°F with low humidity feels pleasant—no need to over-cool. The company is already manufacturing these systems for big box stores and factories, promising to slash energy use and carbon pollution.

stories.title: This Mom is on a Mission to Change the Future of Food Allergies
stories.summary: After her daughter developed a severe nut allergy, Catherine Jaxon turned frustration into action. She co-founded Mission MightyMe, creating baby puffs that introduce peanuts early to help prevent allergies—and she’s already changing the future of food safety.
stories.extended_summary: When Catherine Jaxon’s third child was born, new research showed that feeding babies peanut foods early could prevent up to 86% of allergies. But there were no safe, baby-friendly options on the shelf. So she created her own: a line of peanut puffs that combine nutrition with allergen introduction. Now Mission MightyMe is helping thousands of families navigate prevention with peace of mind—proving that a mother’s frustration can spark a revolution in pediatric health.

stories.title: This founder was an AI layoff 9 months ago. Then he built an instantly profitable company with 2 partners and 12 agents
stories.summary: Sam Brown was laid off by AI just nine months ago. With $300 and two partners, he built Fathom AI — a sales platform running on 12 AI agents that hit $300k ARR in 12 weeks and is on track for $5 million by year-end.
stories.extended_summary: Sam Brown didn't just lose his job to AI — he turned around and used AI to build something bigger. With $300 and two co-founders, he created Fathom AI, a sales enablement platform for medical aesthetics that runs on 12 AI agents. Within three months, it hit $300,000 in annual recurring revenue with 90% gross margins, and it's projecting $5 million by year-end. The 12 agents do the work of an 8-10 person sales-ops team — a stark inversion of the layoff that started it all.
stories.headline_split: This founder was an AI layoff 9 months ago. Then he built an instantly profitable company with 2 partners and 12
stories.fake_headline: Waitress Receives $10,000 Tip From Regular Customer Who Credits Her For Saving His Marriage

stories.title: Too Scared To Drive The Mackinac Bridge? Michigan Has A Solution
stories.summary: The Mackinac Bridge offers a secret driver assistance program that’s been running since the 1980s. Fearful drivers can request a trained pro to take their car across the five-mile span while they ride shotgun—and yes, it’s still only $10.
stories.extended_summary: The five-mile Mackinac Bridge soars 200 feet above the water, with grated lanes that let you see straight down. For drivers with a fear of heights, it's a nightmare. But Michigan has a fix most people don't know about: the Mackinac Bridge Authority's Driver Assistance Program. Simply pull up to the toll booth, ask for help, and a trained driver will steer your car across while you relax as a passenger. The service, available since the '80s, also covers walkers and bikers—and it only costs ten bucks.

stories.fake_headline: Woman Knits Sweaters for Every Shelter Dog, Helps Them Get Adopted

stories.title: Unwanted lab rats find new homes and are adopted thanks to an Argentine organization
stories.summary: Argentina’s Team Ratas is finding homes for lab rats through events like Ratapalooza. The annual adoption fair helps these intelligent, sweet rodents start new lives as beloved pets instead of research subjects.
stories.extended_summary: Albino rats leap from baskets into cages at Ratapalooza, an annual Buenos Aires event that turns lab animals into family pets. The organization Team Ratas rescues rodents from research facilities and fosters them until they find permanent homes. At the fair, stalls sell rat-themed merchandise while adopters share stories of their new companions. María Gabriela Aponte adopted three lab rats and says they're incredibly intelligent and affectionate. It's a second chance for animals usually overlooked—and a powerful reminder that every life deserves love.
stories.headline_split: Unwanted lab rats find new homes and are adopted thanks to
stories.fake_headline: Community Rallies to Save Historic Theater, Raises $500,000 in One Week

stories.title: ‘A Special Place’: Udall pickleball players get bargain from city council
stories.summary: Tucson's Udall Park pickleball players rallied against a proposed hourly fee—and won. The city council voted unanimously to let the community group maintain the courts in exchange for free play, keeping the sport accessible to all.
stories.extended_summary: Leonard Finkel spends more waking hours at the pickleball courts than at home—so when Tucson considered charging $3.50 per 90-minute play, he knew something had to change. The Tucson Area Pickleball (TAP) group negotiated a deal: they'd take over court maintenance, surface cleaning, and new nets, while the city keeps the shade structures and water fountains. The council unanimously approved the MOU, preserving a democratic system where players of all levels can join in—no fee required.

stories.fake_headline: Retired Teacher Opens Free Tutoring Center in Underserved Neighborhood

stories.title: 53 names, finally spoken: Woodberry student’s research restores recognition to enslaved individuals who made the school possible
stories.summary: Woodberry Forest student Jayden Crosby-Brewer researched 53 enslaved individuals who lived on the school's land. His work will be honored at a commemoration ceremony, restoring names and dignity to those whose labor made the school possible.
stories.extended_summary: Inspired by a graduation mention, Jayden Crosby-Brewer spent two years excavating the lives of 53 people enslaved by William Madison. He partnered with historians and uncovered records that gave each person a name and story. On May 16, Woodberry Forest will publicly recognize them, a step toward acknowledging the school's complete history. 'It's about giving them the respect they deserve,' Jayden says.
stories.headline_split: 53 names, finally spoken: Woodberry student’s research restores recognition to
fake_endings: forgotten veterans of World War I
native American leaders in the region
stories.fake_headline: Eighth Grader Discovers New Species of Beetle in Backyard, Cataloged by Smithsonian

stories.title: A new life for old landfills: Indian Lake project turns capped site into pollinator meadow
stories.summary: A capped landfill in New York's Adirondacks is being turned into an eight-acre pollinator meadow. The project shows how old dumps can become vital habitat for bees and butterflies while benefiting the community.
stories.extended_summary: For years, a closed landfill in Indian Lake sat as a grassy mound—unused and forgotten. Now, through a partnership with AdkAction, the site is blooming into a pollinator meadow with native plants that attract bees, butterflies, and other essential insects. The three-year demonstration project aims to replicate this transformation on over 100 capped landfills across the Adirondacks, turning environmental liabilities into ecological assets. With monarch butterflies in decline, this creative reuse offers a model for how waste sites can become wildlife havens.
stories.headline_split: A new life for old landfills: Indian Lake project turns capped site into
stories.fake_headline: Artist Transforms Abandoned Factory Into Affordable Housing for Artists

stories.title: At K&A, a free grocery store in Kensington gives shoppers 20 points twice a month
stories.summary: Greater Goods in Kensington gives shoppers 20 points twice a month to choose their own groceries—no income checks, just a photo ID. It restores dignity and tackles food insecurity in a neighborhood where fresh food is scarce.
stories.extended_summary: Every morning, a line forms outside Greater Goods. Inside, customers fill carts using points instead of dollars—milk costs 3 points, eggs 2. No pre-packed bags, no income verification. Just a photo ID. 'It restores dignity,' says director Melanie Beddis. Founded by the Sunday Love Project, the store sits in a food desert. The model is simple: treat people like customers, not charity. The result is a community that feels respected and fed. 'We don't care if your ID is from another country,' Beddis says. 'We just want you to have what you need.'
stories.headline_split: At K&A, a free grocery store in Kensington gives shoppers 20 points
stories.fake_headline: Community Center Opens Free Bike Repair Station, Teaches Maintenance Skills

stories.title: Augusta University Breaks Ground on Savannah Dental Campus to Address Georgia Provider Shortage
stories.summary: Augusta University breaks ground on a new dental campus in Savannah to train 50 dentists and 20 hygienists per year, addressing Georgia's severe shortage. The state ranks 46th in dentists per capita, with 18 counties having none. The $178 million facility opens in 2028.
stories.extended_summary: Georgia has only one dental school — for 57 years. Now, Augusta University is building a second campus in Savannah to train 50 more dentists and 20 hygienists annually. The $178 million building on Georgia Southern's Armstrong Campus comes as coastal Georgia's population is expected to grow 20-30% this decade. Nearly one-fifth of the current school's 60,000 annual patients already drive from Savannah to Augusta. The new campus will create over 400 jobs and generate $2.8 million in state revenue each year. Classes start summer 2028.
stories.headline_split: Augusta University Breaks Ground on Savannah Dental Campus to
fake_endings: serve rural communities with mobile units
stories.fake_headline: Atlanta Zoo Welcomes Orphaned Elephant Calf After Cross-Country Rescue

stories.title: Beat The Buzz: South Dakotan Develops Clever Mosquito Trap
stories.summary: Lisa Rathbun from South Dakota invented the 'Mosquito Bucket of Doom' — a DIY trap using stagnant water, decomposing leaves, and a BTI tablet that kills mosquito larvae without harming pets or pollinators. It's cheap, safe, and hilariously named.
stories.extended_summary: Lisa Rathbun calls her creation the 'Mosquito Bucket of Doom,' and yes, it's exactly as wonderfully morbid as it sounds. Fill a bucket with standing water, toss in some decomposing grass clippings, and add a mosquito dunk containing BTI — a naturally occurring bacterium that kills larvae but leaves people, pets, and pollinators untouched. The gunk releases CO₂, luring female mosquitoes to lay their eggs there. Plot twist: it's a one-way trip to Doomsville. Just don't put the bucket near your patio unless you're donating blood. The whole setup costs pennies and works like a charm.
stories.headline_split: Beat The Buzz: South Dakotan Develops
stories.fake_headline: Local Teen Creates App to Connect Senior Citizens with Volunteer Tutors

stories.title: Bubble of Love, Shug and Dogg of Women’s Weekend
stories.summary: Women's Weekend in Guerneville was on the brink of collapse three years ago, with only 200 attendees. Now it's back with 2,000 expected, thanks to two local producers who saved the sapphic tradition.
stories.extended_summary: Just three years ago, the 50-year-old Women's Weekend in Guerneville had shrunk to 200 guests. Then producers Jodi Goldstein and Tressa Young—aka Miss Shugana and Madd Dogg 20/20—stepped in to revive it. This year, the event is projected to draw 2,000 visitors from 22 states and three countries, with drag shows, bike rides, and a 'love bubble' atmosphere. In a time when queer spaces feel fragile, this comeback proves that community can rally and grow when it matters most.
stories.headline_split: Bubble of Love, Shug and Dogg of
stories.fake_headline: Elementary School Students Write Letters to Nursing Home Residents, Sparking Lifelong Friendships

stories.title: Changing Lives through Rugby: The D1 Rugby Program at ARC
stories.summary: American River College's rugby team has gone undefeated in California for three straight years and is ranked second nationally. Head coach Zach Swithenbank started the D1 program to give overlooked students a second chance — 33 players have already moved on to universities, certificates, or careers.
stories.extended_summary: The American River College rugby team didn't exist three years ago. Now they're undefeated in California for three consecutive years, 7-0 in conference play this season, and ranked #2 in the country. Head coach Zach Swithenbank started the D1 program because he saw too many doors close on student-athletes — either they were overlooked in high school, couldn't afford a four-year university, or weren't mature enough yet. 'This gives them an opportunity,' he says. Already, 33 players have moved on to D1 universities, earned certificates, or entered the workforce. The team's success on the field is matched by the lives it's changed off it.
stories.headline_split: Changing Lives through Rugby: The D1 Rugby Program at
stories.fake_headline: Wheelchair Basketball Team Wins National Championship in Dramatic Overtime

stories.title: Cimrman in English: Translating Humor
stories.summary: A Prague English theater group has spent twelve years translating the famously untranslatable plays of Czech legend Jara Cimrman. Their efforts have brought absurdist Czech humor to international audiences—and it works.
stories.extended_summary: When British expat Brian Stewart first watched a Cimrman play, he laughed despite not understanding Czech. That spark led him and his partner to translate the works of Czechoslovakia's greatest fictional playwright for English audiences. Over twelve years, they've tackled six plays full of absurdity—like a retired actor who can't remember which play he's in. The productions have become a hit among expats and locals alike, proving that great humor transcends language.
stories.headline_split: Cimrman in English:
stories.fake_headline: Local Man Breaks World Record for Longest Hug, Raises Funds for Charity

stories.title: Communities to Earn Fertilizer for Planting Trees Around Lake Malawi National Park
stories.summary: In Malawi, a community-led initiative rewards households with fertilizer for successfully growing and maintaining trees around Lake Malawi National Park. By requiring trees to reach 1.5 meters, the program addresses the common challenge of nurturing saplings, encouraging long-term conservation and reducing pressure on forest resources.
stories.extended_summary: Households that plant and care for 15 different tree species around Lake Malawi National Park can earn two 50-kilogram bags of fertilizer once the trees hit 1.5 meters. The incentive, launched by Sustainable Cape Maclear, aims to solve the perennial problem of trees being planted but not cared for. Regular monitoring ensures trees survive, and community leaders and park officials hope this motivates lasting stewardship and eases strain on protected forests.

stories.title: Dancer with ALS Performs on Stage Again Through Digital Avatar–WATCH
stories.summary: A dancer with ALS returned to the stage through a digital avatar controlled by her brain. Breanna Olson projected her imagination into a mixed reality performance in Amsterdam, proving that even when the body fails, art can still move.
stories.extended_summary: Breanna Olson, a dancer confined to a wheelchair by ALS, imagined dancing again — and thanks to a brain interface from Dentsu Lab, her digital avatar pranced across a stage in Amsterdam. The technology translated her brain signals into dance moves, giving her a moment she never dreamed possible. 'It was just a beautiful and memorable moment,' she said.

stories.title: Detroit Advocates Push for Citywide Composting After Pilot Diverts 3,300 Pounds of Food Scraps
stories.summary: A three-month composting pilot in Detroit kept over 3,300 pounds of food scraps out of landfills. Now advocates are pushing to expand the program citywide, with drop-off sites in every district.
stories.extended_summary: The Detroit Community Composting Collective Project trained 75 backyard composters who diverted more than 3,300 pounds of food scraps from August to November. Now, advocates like Ricky Blanding of Scrap Soils are proposing mandatory composting in city buildings and drop-off locations across all council districts. The city's Office of Sustainability has already announced expansion after a separate nine-month pilot. With current regulations treating composters as solid waste handlers, changes are needed to make composting accessible—and the community is leading the charge.

stories.title: Detroit schools are making real gains in reading and math: 4 strategies that are working
stories.summary: Detroit schools are seeing real academic gains thanks to strategies like targeted reading intervention funded by a legal settlement. In one classroom, a first grader named Marvin gets personalized help spelling words, reflecting a district-wide effort to address past failures and improve literacy.
stories.extended_summary: With five first graders watching, interventionist Aja Penick helped 7-year-old Marvin correct his spelling of 'same' through patient questioning, a scene playing out in Detroit schools thanks to a $94 million settlement. The funds, from a 2016 lawsuit over students' right to read, have paid for 18 interventionists at Munger Elementary alone. The district's focus on evidence-based strategies is yielding measurable progress in reading and math, offering hope after years of neglect.
stories.headline_split: Detroit schools are making real gains in reading and math:
fake_endings: a focus on early literacy and math
stories.fake_headline: Teacher in Rural Alaska Uses Dog Sledding to Teach Math, Attendance Skyrockets

stories.title: Field Museum hosts NASCAR-Themed STEAM Fest for CPS
stories.summary: Over 550 Chicago students competed in NASCAR-themed challenges at the Field Museum, connecting classroom lessons to real-world motorsports careers. Top performers won tickets to the Independence Day race weekend.
stories.extended_summary: More than 22,000 CPS students initially competed in-class design challenges to earn a spot at the Field Museum's fourth annual STEAM Fest. The 550 winners faced off in engineering tasks alongside professional drivers like Lanie Buice and pit crew coaches. The event showed students that science, technology, and math can lead to exciting careers in motorsports. Chicagoland Speedway's general manager says it's about inspiring the next generation—and for many students, the day was a thrilling glimpse into a future they hadn't considered.
